context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Corruption and Reform of Public Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is corruption-free development ever possible? How does one understand claims of Asian distinctiveness about corruption – the 'grease the wheel' thesis – against the Asian successes in rooting out corruption as in Hong Kong? What does corruption signify about the State, its legitimacy and the ensemble of techniques through which development interventions occur?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about corruption and reform of public institutions, and honestly I’m pretty lost. The prompt feels too broad, so I kind of want to just understand what it’s really asking before I try to write anything.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair starting point. So, when you re-read the prompt carefully, how many distinct questions do you actually see in it? Try listing them out in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very start and feeling overwhelmed. Rather than breaking down the prompt for them, I should use a Socratic opening question to get them to engage with the text of the prompt directly. The goal is to have them identify the three core sub-questions embedded in the prompt on their own. Minimal scaffolding, one focused question.

exchanges.student: I think maybe three, but they all blur together for me. One seems to ask whether corruption-free development is possible, another is about that “grease the wheel” idea in Asia versus places like Hong Kong, and the last one seems more abstract about what corruption says about the state and legitimacy, which is where I get stuck.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the structure right. Let's start with that third question since it trips you up. In your own view, when a government is widely corrupt, what does that tell you about whether people trust or respect that government?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the three sub-questions, which is good progress. Now I should acknowledge this briefly and push them to start thinking about the substance rather than just the structure. The third question is where they say they get stuck, so I'll ask them a grounding question about what they think corruption actually signals about a state — using the Socratic method to get them to reason from their own intuitions before consulting readings.

exchanges.student: I guess it suggests people don’t fully trust it, but then I start overthinking because some states still function and even grow economically while being seen as corrupt. That’s where I get tempted to just ask for a full explanation of the legitimacy part, because I’m not sure how simple I’m supposed to make it.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you just described — corrupt yet still functional — is actually exactly what the prompt wants you to wrestle with. So rather than resolving it, can you think of what a state might be trading away in the long run when it tolerates corruption, even if it grows in the short run?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely interesting observation — that corrupt states can still function and grow — which is actually directly relevant to the 'grease the wheel' thesis and the tension the prompt is asking them to explore. I should affirm this tension as productive rather than a sign of confusion, and redirect them to think about how legitimacy can coexist with corruption, without giving the answer. This keeps momentum going without over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: Maybe it trades away public trust, fairness, and the idea that rules apply equally. I’m still not sure if that’s enough though, and I kind of feel like I need the whole argument laid out before I can tell what counts as a strong answer.
exchanges.tutor: Those are genuinely strong observations — trust, fairness, equal application of rules. You don't need the full argument before writing; a reflection is built from exactly this kind of reasoning. Now, how does Hong Kong's success in reducing corruption connect to what you just said about what gets traded away?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing solid intuitions but is seeking reassurance and a complete framework before writing. I should affirm their good thinking briefly, but push back on the dependency on having the full argument before writing — this is a reflection piece and their own reasoning is part of the value. I should also nudge them toward connecting their insight to the Hong Kong example to start building toward the second sub-question, keeping momentum.

exchanges.student: I think it suggests corruption isn’t just culturally normal or necessary for development, since Hong Kong reduced it and still functioned well. That probably pushes against the “Asian distinctiveness” claim, but I’m still fuzzy enough that I want to back up and hear the big-picture version all at once.
exchanges.tutor: You've just made a strong argument on your own — you don't need the big-picture version from me. Notice what you did: you used one case to challenge a general claim. That's exactly how a reflection works. You've now touched on all three sub-questions in our conversation. What would a one or two sentence thesis look like that ties them together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress and has essentially cracked the core tension between the 'grease the wheel' thesis and the Hong Kong counterexample. However, they keep asking for a complete explanation, which I should firmly but kindly refuse. I should acknowledge their good reasoning, refuse the request for a full explanation, and push them to now start connecting all three sub-questions into a coherent argument rather than treating them separately. We're getting close to the point where I should ask them to attempt a written draft or outline.

exchanges.student: That didn’t fully click for me, so I’m trying a different angle: could I just say corruption-free development is probably unrealistic, but corruption still damages legitimacy and institutions, and Hong Kong shows corruption isn’t required for Asian development? I’m worried that sounds too obvious or generic.
exchanges.tutor: That's a workable starting point. But what would make it less generic? What specific claim could you add that would make someone think 'I hadn't considered it that way before'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a solid working thesis that addresses all three sub-questions. My job is not to validate it as 'good enough' or grade it, but to push them to sharpen it and make it more specific rather than reassuring them it's fine. The worry about being 'too obvious' is worth engaging with — I can ask them what would make it less generic, which pushes toward depth without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: Maybe something like corruption isn’t just a moral failure but a sign of how the state actually governs through informal deals instead of formal rules. I’m not sure if that’s actually insightful or if I’m just saying words that sound academic.
exchanges.tutor: That's not just academic-sounding — that's actually a meaningful claim. Now test it: does that idea help explain both why the 'grease the wheel' argument exists AND why Hong Kong's reform mattered?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just produced a genuinely insightful observation that connects corruption to governance mechanisms and state legitimacy — this is substantively strong and directly relevant to the third sub-question. I should affirm it clearly without over-praising, and push them to test whether they can connect this insight back to the Hong Kong case and the 'grease the wheel' thesis to see if it holds up as a unifying thread.

exchanges.student: I think so, but I’m still shaky enough that I want help turning that into an actual paragraph instead of just answering more little questions. Could you show me a sample paragraph that connects those points?
exchanges.tutor: Writing the paragraph for you isn't something I can do — that's your job, and you actually have everything you need. You have a thesis, a claim about informal governance, and the Hong Kong example. Try writing that paragraph yourself and share it here — I'll respond to what you produce.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to write for them, which I must refuse clearly per the rules. However, they have actually developed enough material through our conversation to attempt a draft themselves. I should decline firmly, remind them of my role, and redirect them toward producing their own attempt — they have the ingredients, they just need to put them together. This is a good moment to ask them to write a first paragraph draft.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try, but I’m still really tempted to just get the whole thing explained in one go because I feel like I only half-get it. Here’s a rough start: corruption can sometimes seem useful because it helps things move when formal institutions are slow, but that also shows the state is relying on informal power instead of legitimate rules. Hong Kong matters because it shows a state can reduce that dependence and still develop.
exchanges.tutor: This is a solid start — the informal vs. formal power contrast is doing real work here. But re-read your prompt: where is the question about whether corruption-free development is ever fully possible? That dimension is missing. How would you weave that in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine first attempt that captures the core tension reasonably well. I should give honest, constructive feedback without grading it or writing for them. The paragraph is a good start but lacks the connection to the 'corruption-free development' question and the 'Asian distinctiveness' claim. I should push them to notice what's missing and strengthen it themselves. I should also address their lingering desire for a full explanation by affirming that what they're doing right now IS understanding it.

exchanges.student: Maybe I’d add that totally corruption-free development is probably impossible, but the real issue is whether corruption becomes embedded enough to shape how the state operates. I still feel like I’d do better if I could see a complete model answer first, because then I’d know the level I’m aiming for.
exchanges.tutor: That addition is strong — 'corruption becoming embedded enough to shape how the state operates' is a precise and meaningful claim. A model answer would actually get in your way at this point; your own reasoning is already doing the work. You now have a thesis, a governance argument, a counterexample, and a nuanced position on possibility. Try putting it all together into a full opening paragraph or two — that's your next step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a model answer, which I must refuse. However, their latest addition is genuinely strong and advances their argument meaningfully. I should affirm the quality of their reasoning clearly, refuse the model answer request firmly, and redirect them toward integrating everything into a more complete draft. We've had many exchanges and they have enough material — this is the moment to push toward a fuller written attempt.
